title: Viviane Ramos -Panel Interview | Engagement Manager (Account Management)
meeting_title: Viviane Ramos -Panel Interview | Engagement Manager (Account Management)

transcript.text: Hello.
Oh, you're muted.
Second time I do that, huh?
How are you?
I'm doing well.
How are you?
I'm doing well as well.
Thank you for asking.
Oh, man.
It's the most frustrating thing when you're like, and you realize you've been talking to a ghost.
I know.
I know.
It's frustrating on both ends.
Mm-hmm.
I have one client that, like, just, it absolutely drives him crazy, and every time he gets on a call, it just, it's always auto-mute when you have, like, four or five people on the call.
Oh, man.
He just, his anger boils over.
I'm like, I don't know how to remove this.
Morning.
Good morning.
Hey.
Morning, George.
Nice to meet you.
I'm Vivian.
Good to meet you as well.
I heard we're talking about tough clients.
You know, it's the topic of every day.
No, no, no.
was sillier than that.
It's like, every time he gets on, he's auto-muted.
And it, like, pisses him off so badly.
It's, like, overly angry about it.
But, yeah.
So George is our chief of staff.
He's, like, an incredibly capable all-purpose, does everything.
He really helped me a lot in the beginning.
So I'm excited to have him on this call, too.
Well, that's exciting.
We were supposed to meet 101 first, but then got moved straight into the panel.
That's a good sign, though, yeah.
William had great things to say.
So excited to dive into the presentation and, you know, learn more about you.
And, yeah, super excited.
It's, like, one of the most important roles right now at the company because it, like, sets excellence for every client that we have.
But it's also an ever-evolving process of our technology catching up to what we actually do for clients and to our product.
And so it's super interesting, super complex.
Max Williams in the heart of it right now leading that.
Sprint team, so he knows, but yeah, super excited to get to hear more.
Yeah, yeah, me I think we're waiting on one last person, and then we can get started.
Got it, got it.
There she is.
I think Bridget's just joining.
Hi, I'm sorry I'm late.
You're fine.
No worries.
Nice meet you.
Nice to meet you.
So should we dive in right away?
Sure.
Let's do it.
All right.
I will go ahead and share my screen.
We will ask questions throughout.
I don't know if they told you that, but we'll probably interrupt you, so you can expect that.
That's what I was going to ask.
I don't know if you prefer questions at the end.
I'm fine with interruptions.
Perfect.
Yeah, feel free to interrupt me as I go.
All right.
For articles in eight weeks, willing to be transparent.
I don't recall the exact number that you had told me, but that felt feasible, three articles a week, always thinking of the quality in mind.
And this client, they are at a really mature stage already.
They are organic landscape, so that felt like a good number, excuse me.
How do we measure the success beyond article production, increase in impression, and CTR, since the idea is to have those terms ranking at the top across those prioritized pages.
And then I've also seen an increase in AI overviews and LLM citations, as I mentioned.
Any questions so far?
Yeah, this was good.
Okay, so like I said, I want...
I to keep this as basic as possible because it was different than what I felt when I started diving into the site.
Ramp is really mature.
They have, I think, within the thousands of blog articles.
I know I got two sheets for this exercise.
And I knew Bracks before, and I knew Bracks had good organic presence, but I had no idea I was going to see Ramp had, you know, twice as better presence than the client.
So instead of creating new content, the idea is to expand and go deeper into existing content.
So content that's highly relevant to the personas, to the product, and that is dated, there is some opportunity.
So we're talking about articles from 2022.

Even earlier, and then doing a refresh, a rewrite, and then also expanding into a cluster that talks a little bit about thought leadership, but also has a decent search volume behind, but really is more focused on those decision makers.
And it was actually a gap that I found for RAMP.
It was a little bit tough in the beginning because my brain always wants to look for gaps.

You know, that's one of the fastest ways that we can build authority, but when we are working with a mature client like this, after a while researching when I actually found a gap and gaps in rankings, I was very excited.
That was going to actually be one of my questions is like,
It makes sense to me that your focus is on optimizations because, like you said, it's an extremely mature, not only a mature client, but it is the leader in that space from an organic perspective.
But I was curious if there were any gaps that you had found and you kind of answered that question of like net news.
What I'm hearing you say, correct me if I'm wrong, is that like that's not really worth it as much as really focusing on optimizations.
Is that correct?
That is correct.

And then part of this strategy is me thinking about us recycling these existing articles and getting them to perform better within the short term, but also within the long term, as we start exploring a new cluster, it also has the time to create authority.
It's already close to the topics about CFOs.
I don't know if you had a chance to look at the presentation or so it's not like the authority just doesn't exist, is that the topic was not really explored.
I found with competitors.
So like I said, the strategy really is identifying more pages that are within striking distance.
So position four to 20 and optimizing those for the top rankings, improving this cluster authority.
It already exists.
So for procurement and the other topic that I selected, sorry, I have to cheat and look at the cluster.
It's a finance operations.
It's really, really mature.
But there is a lot of opportunity for us to get better in terms of performance there.
And then expand selectively, expanding into the CFO level strategy with a content that is, it feels a little bit more like thought leadership talking about decision frameworks.
Product-led insights, but also sharing an opinion, and that it has that expertise that is required for a client like Rem.
So the clusters that I came up with are finance operations.
Like I said, I'm giving an example here for a high-intent keyword, which is spend optimization.
I found an article about that term that we have the opportunity to optimize and improve it.
And it's also really aligned with Rem's value proposition, optimizing for spend.
And then we'll also update the supporting articles for that term.
So I think that would be a really good start.
This is not 100% a gap.
The enterprise...

procurement, because there is a cluster that talks about the subject, but it's not really developed thinking about enterprises, and there is a small opportunity for that, and room to deepen the existing content that we have.

And then finally, for CFOs, and Brax, the main competitor, has a section of the website, I believe it's called the CFO Corner, so they put out a ton of content, I guess, like really stepping into the shoes of the CFO and writing with a ton of empathy towards that, target audience, I believe this is an opportunity for us to build a similar narrative here.
And then, again, the search volume might be a bit lower.
But it's extremely relevant for the audience.
Quick question about that.
What, in your opinion, is the benefit of adding a lot of expertise to content production?
That's part one.
And then part two is, how would you go about working with a client to get internal experts to help you produce the content?
Okay, two questions.
So, RAMSA, your money, your life website, there is a major EAT component.
So, expertise is mandatory.
I don't have authors.
It's not always the type of author we would like to see.
They have marketing managers writing financial content, which is common.
But they also have people within their finance department.
So, yeah, that's why it's important.
And second, yeah.
That is something that needs client alignment.
To be honest, that's a really good question.
I don't know if our editors have finance expertise or topical expertise.
I know sometimes that is the case.

And if they do, it could be the case that, you know, they remain as the ghostwriter and we have someone that's high visibility within the client team, kind of lending their authority by posting their photo in their profile.
I've worked with SMEs in the past when I was in-house with a finance company, and it's very hard to get their time to review articles.
So I understand that's not always possible, but there are ways for us to work around it.
Okay, great.
Cool.
So these are some of the examples that I've put together for...
What article execution would look like for CFO terms, we have three different ones.
The intent is informational for all, but the idea is really to step into their shoes.
I think this angle of the 90-day CFO playbook can do quite well with this audience, especially newer CFOs, KPIs.
That they should track, you know, sometimes looking for inspiration.
There would probably be other ways of distributing this content other than just organic.
So I think this type of content has a lot of potential.
Also because of the potential backlinks, it can create and have CFOs like bookmarking the website and revisiting.
Finally, we have CFO priorities.
I think this think this is a a a
Lower search volume, but this is a piece that the client would be able to refresh every year and kind of create that anticipation with the audience.
You know, I'm hopeful it could become like a large piece that people look forward to reading every year.
Then moving on to a commercial topic, it's a spend optimization, like I mentioned before.

So repurposing that framework, a step-by-step model within that finance operations cluster, and then enterprise procurement, how to scale and how to manage it in complex multi-departments that are spending.
So I think this could also resonate quite well with the audience.
This is my last slide.
So the way I'm answering this, to be transparent, is kind of like how I work with AI today.
You shared some data with me.

I used AI to analyze the ranking distribution, and that's how I would continue to use it, to understand where the opportunities are, to help me with selecting the clusters, so filling out thousands of rows that it would take me, you know, a week to do so maybe, and then kind of validate the strategic ideas before I commit to clusters, pressure testing some ideas.
And then, of course, we have a human editor, in the case of this presentation, it was me.
So checking that everything was correct, but for this audience, really...
A little bit I use my own, I like to do manual work and browse the site, that's the way I can get a feel for it, not just looking at spreadsheets and URL names and title tags and so on.
So I use ChatGPT to categorize the clusters, break it down like percentages, opportunities, that kind of stuff.

And then I manually looked for gaps using SEMrush, looked at A-drafts, a little bit of SERP research, and then like a lot of, honestly, I've always looked like this, but a lot of browsing to kind of website browsing to form an opinion.
William knows this, I'm an SEO, so this was a little bit tricky for me to fill out.
I know with GrowthX, we're talking about agents, that's not something.
It's I have a ton of experience with, but I use AI every day.
So, yeah, instead of me doing data manipulation, I used AI to set that up for me.
Does that answer your question?
Yep, yep.
Thank you.
I have a question, but I want to, Bridget, did you have something on your mind?
I don't want to be the hogging all the time.
Yeah, yeah, I'll ask a quick one.
So in your second slide, I think you had three success metrics you were going to look at.
Yes, perfect.
Did you get a chance to look at, like, how they're doing in the third bucket right now?
I didn't.
I didn't.
It's something that I overlooked, to be honest, but I would assume they are not doing bad considering their SEO presence.
That's an assumption.
How would you, okay, if they, if they, or how would you balance, like...

Going for citation wins over, like, if they came to you and said, we really want to win in, like, AEO, but we also care so much about SEO, like, how would you try to balance that in that work execution?
That's a good question.
I think that we are not writing content anymore without thinking about LLMs.
So that's all about the structure.
So I feel like that is part of the process of us writing new content already.
We're already writing with LLMs and AI overviews in mind.
So we're not choosing one versus the other, if you will.
Mm-hmm.
Yeah.
Okay.
I have a broader question.
Looking at all this, what, let me see, I wrote it down.
Um, okay.
Yes.
Basically, do you think that Ramp, based off of this work, do you think that Ramp would be an easy client or a difficult client, and why?
Oh.
In what way?
Do you mean client management?
No, no, no.
I mean specifically in getting results.
I should have clarified that, yeah.
Like, you get this as a client, and they have expectations.
Do you think this is an easy client, a hard client, and if so, why?
Great question.
I don't want to say it's easy.
I want to say it's easier than a domain with no authority.
I think it gives us the opportunity to try things out, like the CFO cluster, for example.
I do feel like that could be something that the impact is not felt immediately, but I feel that we'd
Some optimization of existing clusters and dated articles, we could feel the lift right away.
So, yes, I think we could see some results faster and then more, but within the long run.
Okay.
How are you, just curious, kind of like a segue, but we're separate, but how are you staying on top of like AI visibility, like how fast everything's changing?

Obviously, you know, the fundamentals are still required, the SEO fundamentals, but just curious on like how you're staying up to date, how you're learning about AEO, how you're, what techniques you're incorporating.
If any, just genuinely curious, because it's moving so fast, so.
It really is.
I try not to obsess over anything.
I think.
The basics are there, but I do weekly readings on SEO and, you know, general AI optimization.
But what I did was last year, I did a couple of deep dives to see how we could improve performance and then come up with recommendations that I currently use in my work.
So it's really nothing new what we're doing, I think, when doing keyword research, for example, be a little bit more focused on questions, right?
So always making sure we are answering questions in a good way when it comes to technical aspects.
I've always defended schema markup and good technical hygiene, but I think that's really helpful right now, the markup.
LLMs.
Then there is a lot of debate in our industry that AI visibility should also be a brand scope because when we're talking about citations, we're talking about brand management and PR and those things.
So I think it's important that SEO doesn't carry the weight alone.
And for us to always understand that it's not just SEO that moves the needle.
If it's a brand that's starting overnight, that authority needs to be created from the ground up.
And it's not a single channel that's going to do that.
Although a very powerful one.
Yeah, that's spot on.
I'm curious if you have experience.
This is honestly just out of curiosity, honestly, in backlinking.
And in that space of trying to do kind of the off-page stuff.
I've never done outreach, if that's your question.
But I work with agencies.
I work with one at the moment.
I build content thinking about backlinks.
I still believe that's a thing, that good content will naturally create good backlinks.
Our chief content officer agrees with you.
He's like, don't do backlinking, just create good content that people will naturally backlink.
Yeah, yeah, apparently.
Yeah, I like to think that way.
But, I mean, there are so many agencies that, I don't know, they're doing this for 20 years and they're going and they have a ton of clients.
So, I mean, clearly it works.
Yeah.
I, because I, we didn't, I didn't meet you in a different interview.
I'm just curious, like.
you.
Thank thank
So what in your past roles or maybe in the most recent ones, like what is the normal interaction you have with editors and how do you typically work with them?
Like it's two different roles, obviously, in our company.
So I'm just curious what's how you normally work with them.
Are they in-house?
they using freelancers or what do you typically do?
I've worked with them in-house mostly.
So I was at Graphite for almost three years.
You might know of them because they have that distinction of the editorial team and the account management growth team.
But yeah, we reviewed each other's work.
It's like there was a workflow, but it was a separate team.
Right now when I'm working with editors and copywriters, it's in my current company.
So I still try to meet with them and keep the conversation open.
I think it's important to stay in touch and make sure that there are no gaps in the communication since it's such an important role.
What stands out to you about a really good editor versus a mediocre one?
Oh, good question.
I think it all boils down to the content is the content excellent to read and is a ranking.
So I'm thinking about the outcome here.
Yeah.
And I mean, I've worked with people who are very attached to the writing, what they're saying, you know, so an editor that understands that the copy needs to be search friendly.
It's also a good editor, in my opinion.
mean, are there other, because like, obviously the results.
people have Yes.
Yes.
Would be like a lagging indicator if someone's doing a good job.
Is there anything earlier on like leading indicators of someone's like going to produce something that will have results?
Does that make sense?
It makes sense.
Yeah, I think honestly, Bridget, that is like a personality thing, like this type of role that they're doing.
So it's someone who is deeply engaged in the role, has vast knowledge of a variety of things and can pour that into the work.
I'm thinking now like as an interviewer, what I would be looking for in a good editor.
Yeah, that's what curious about.

But the people that I've worked with, and yeah, this is probably not the answer you're looking for, but the editors that I worked with, they had like such interesting lives and like interests outside of work, you know, like probably reading a lot, doing a lot of things.
I
I think that's what makes them good.
Okay.
One of the things.
So very well-rounded people, basically.
Exactly.
red people.
Exactly.
Yeah, well-rounded is a good word.
That's interesting.
It's an observation, really.
One of the things I was curious about is one of the core things in the exercise was what you learned.
So what you learned about Ramp's site, the market, constraints they're assuming.
You talked to us a little bit about that at the beginning of kind of them being twice the size of Brex, et cetera.
But in the process, let's say you have a new client and you're like, I need to learn about these guys in 48 hours.
Like, what does that look like for you when you're like, okay, I got this new client.
Now I need to like absorb a ton of information about this client.
What is your typical...
Yeah, like I said to George, so I like to browse and read a lot of pages and it's kind of like go browsing without a specific direction.
And I like to do that on my time off as well sometimes when there is no pressure off other exercises.
So I did this over on the weekend, of course, understanding the brief and will read documentation that has been provided.
But even doing things such as like an LLM search, like a teach me something and then browsing.
I am not in North America at the moment, but I know Bracks.
Like I already knew Bracks because I have a friend that works for them.
Yeah, so I think that that helps, you know, like already knowing the landscape a little bit.
different утites.
If it's something that I don't know about it at all, then that is a bit tougher.
But in this case, like I already knew a little bit about like the product, the industry, the competition.
Yeah, I I didn't know anything.
I've learned a few things like Brax being acquired and then all the strategies that Ramp does to collect leads, which is amazing.
Like they're very aggressive.
So, yeah, those are some of the things that caught my attention.
Yeah, so obviously, like assuming most of the people you're going to or clients you're going to work with are ones that you really know nothing about.

You don't have any background of what I'm hearing you say is a focus on just navigating around their site, their digital presence without any kind of agenda, just kind of passively absorbing and seeing what you're what you're picking up from them.
Number one.
And then number two, using CHADGPT or SAMLLM to do a deep research for you and give you kind of a brief about them.
Those are kind of the two main ways that you research a client.
Yeah, that works really well for me to absorb information.
Got it.
Cool.
You sound like you're pretty familiar with the space and with competitors like Brex, I guess.
Was there anything that was particularly challenging or time-consuming with this assignment?
mean, like what was the most, the piece with the most friction or the most challenging?
Yeah.
So, well, like I said, my brain goes to SEO and wants to find gaps.
It can be very frustrating when you're looking for a theme where your client doesn't rank because RAMP has an incredible presence.
So, I probably looked at 10 different themes that I was like, whoa, this seems like an opportunity and they were there.
great.
You know, so that's why I said I celebrated when I found the CFO gap.
I think that was the main thing for me.
And then, yeah, just seeing how explicit they are in terms of lead generation.
So they have a bunch of comparison pages, Ramp versus Brax, and not just versus, but like they go beyond.
They really expanded.
But I also seen the CTAs, are you a Brax client?
We'll give you $150 off if you, you know, fill out this form right now.
And then another $300 if you sign with us.
I personally have never seen a strategy like that.
Really aggressive.
I thought it was really interesting.
I'm curious, do you have any background with CRO at all?
Have you touched on it?
A little bit.
A
Like a company like this, or we're doing work like this.
Yeah, thank you for that question.
So, yeah, I found myself excited about the assignment, meeting the team, reading about the company, what you guys are doing.
I think it would be a good fit for me.
I'm really interested in the subject and passionate about search.
It feels like a natural next step for me.
I had spoken with Tucker last year and it was not the right time.
He came back to me this year and I feel like SEO can be like a pretty isolating role sometimes.
I think being surrounded of people doing the same job as me and learn together would be a lot better.
I told William when we had our first call.
So right now I work as a senior SEO specialist.
I do a lot of audits and...
And my role was not even supposed to be client-facing, but every time there is a fire, the team brings me on board.
And I miss that.
I miss that energy.
And then, yeah, I think I would have the opportunity to learn a lot and be working with people that are like-minded.
I have to say, that was part of what also appealed to me at GrowthX in the beginning, because it can be very isolating.
You're often the only SEO person in-house, and it's cool to be able to work with people who are really on the cutting edge and smart.
Yeah, yeah.
I've, like, I've been in tech for, I don't want to make this up, but, like, 14 years, maybe?
Yeah, it's been that long.
So, Bridget, I know you're in Vancouver.
Tucker, like, made sure to tell me, because...
I have Canadian citizenship.
lived in Vancouver for 12 years.
So I'm considering moving back this year.
Right now I'm in southern Brazil.
And I worked for Hootsuite back in the day.
So like, you know, I have like that startup energy.
And yeah, looking back, those were like really fun times before my SEO days.
Nice.
Well, let me know if you did move back.
Where did you live in Vancouver?
Kitsilano.
Where are you based?
I live in Kits, too.
I do.
I love it.
Yeah, I miss Kitsilano.
Yeah.
Vancouver crew over here.
Are you in Vancouver as well?
No, no, no, no.
I'm just saying you two.
We have a couple, there's four of us here, or three of us here.
Sydney and Jen also live in, Sydney lives half the time in the Philippines.
A lot of documentation and structure for when myself and another person started about almost three months ago now.
And so there's a lot of great documentation.
So I would say the first week is probably going to be a lot of that, a lot of absorbing.
Also getting training on what our platform looks like, how it works, how managing editors build all of this stuff, really getting insights on that.
And then another core element is learning the, what I would call is like the Marcel way, which is our CEO.
He's really an absolute genius and he's created this incredible system for doing what we do.
And we basically want to learn, want to teach you that structure and make sure that you feel comfortable with it.
You understand all that, you know, all of that, all of that involves.
And then probably the second week or maybe the third week, but probably already the second week, you'd start to shadow myself or one of the other.
DMs to start to like actually observe how meetings are led, how content strategies are built, etc.
But George, please fill in anything I missed.
No, I think it's spot on.

Like context gathering, understanding how things work, the team operates, and then how the strategy and like how we think about everything that we're doing, which is like the Marcel secret sauce, like William said.
And then, yeah, as comfortable as you are beginning to shadow is kind of when we would try to get you there to get the live reps in with the clients.
And then, yeah, that's kind of what the beginning stages look like.
All right.
Well, thank you.
That makes sense.
And then I guess just my last questions about my presentation.
Did you have any feedback?
Like, do you agree with my recommendations?
Did I miss anything obvious?
I don't know if Ramp is your client or someone else.
No, I mean, I saw them made with Gamma.
My actually.
Initial interview for my job here, I made my presentation with Gamma 2 like a year ago when it was way worse.
So I hope this took you, I hope this was quicker than when I made it with Gamma.
But that's cool that you use the tool to help you.
And no, I didn't have too many comments on it, but we have worked with similar clients.
Yeah, I don't think there was anything like specifically missed.
I'm just looking at the assignment again.
I mean, when I first saw this deck, I was like, oh, this is really solid.
I liked the clusters you selected.
I liked the specific titles and topics you selected and the keywords you selected.
I thought it showed a real, yeah, a real depth of expertise in SEO that you were able to immediately go, oh, this is a very mature client.
I need to focus my energy on optimizations, but let me continue.
Continue to dig deeper and see what opportunities we have for, you know, exploration.
Because obviously, like, to some extent, SEO is infinite.
Like, even if it's the biggest company in world, even HubSpot is creating new content every week that's relevant to them, even though they have already so much content.
So that's, I think those are all good things.

I think maybe I would just, yeah, point out what Bridget pointed out, which is like the getting a baseline of AI, you know, AIO or GEO would have been a helpful structure just to have that for yourself mentally is always helpful.
It also depends on if you have access to those tools.
If you don't have Profound or Scrunch or one of those things, then, you know, that can be a bit harder.
But yeah, getting a baseline on that is helpful.
It's not always the easiest to get, though.
I understand that.
Noted.
Thank you very much.
But now you can for free, if you use this link.
Did I just say in the chat?
We launched an AI visibility tool.
Always selling.
And so it's free for you just to like search for brands and see like a general snapshot of their visibility and how they're doing in the competitive space that they're in, in their industry.
So, but it gives you a good snapshot.
But we use this tool and incorporate all of the findings there with everything we're doing on the SEO side to help influence and develop our strategy.
So kind of like to what William said, not definitely not a knock because it wasn't really like that explicit in the assignment either.
But like what clients like to see alongside topic clusters are like prompts or queries that customers are actually asking, the volume.
And then in terms of like articles that are already published, like are they getting mentioned, cited, et cetera.
And like what's working on that in that realm.
But again, it's also new and learnable and easier with tools.
So not a ding.
Amazing.
Thank you.
Yeah, I thought about the tool on LinkedIn.
I'll definitely check it out.
Check out, check that.
Cool.
Awesome.
Thank you, Vivian.
Appreciate all the work and time.
Yeah, thank you.
was great meeting you all.
Awesome.
We'll get back to you soon.
Sounds great.
Bye-bye.
Thanks.
Bye-bye.

default_summary.markdown_formatted:
## Meeting Purpose

[Panel interview for the Engagement Manager role with Viviane Ramos.](https://fathom.video/share/ACsvfUf1TgCwuyF5_AkavALhxpYH_BDk?tab=summary&timestamp=201.0)

## Key Takeaways

  - [**Strategy:** For the mature client Ramp, the strategy blends quick-win optimizations (refreshing old content) with long-term authority building (a new "CFO" cluster).](https://fathom.video/share/ACsvfUf1TgCwuyF5_AkavALhxpYH_BDk?tab=summary&timestamp=357.0)
  - [**Process:** A hybrid research method combines AI for data analysis (e.g., ranking distribution) with manual browsing for qualitative insights and gap validation.](https://fathom.video/share/ACsvfUf1TgCwuyF5_AkavALhxpYH_BDk?tab=summary&timestamp=960.0)
  - [**Fit:** The candidate is motivated by the collaborative environment, seeking to escape the isolation of in-house SEO and leverage 14 years of tech experience.](https://fathom.video/share/ACsvfUf1TgCwuyF5_AkavALhxpYH_BDk?tab=summary&timestamp=2102.0)
  - [**Feedback:** The strategy was praised as solid, but a baseline analysis of AI visibility (AIO/LLM citations) was noted as a valuable addition for future presentations.](https://fathom.video/share/ACsvfUf1TgCwuyF5_AkavALhxpYH_BDk?tab=summary&timestamp=2441.0)

## Topics

### Candidate Motivation & Background

  - [**Experience:** 14 years in tech, including a previous role at Hootsuite.](https://fathom.video/share/ACsvfUf1TgCwuyF5_AkavALhxpYH_BDk?tab=summary&timestamp=2203.0)
  - [**Current Role:** Senior SEO Specialist, but the role is not client-facing.](https://fathom.video/share/ACsvfUf1TgCwuyF5_AkavALhxpYH_BDk?tab=summary&timestamp=2153.0)
  - [**Motivation for GrowthX:** Seeks a collaborative environment to escape the isolation of in-house SEO and apply a passion for search.](https://fathom.video/share/ACsvfUf1TgCwuyF5_AkavALhxpYH_BDk?tab=summary&timestamp=2102.0)
  - [**Personal:** Canadian citizen (lived in Kitsilano, Vancouver) considering a move back from Brazil.](https://fathom.video/share/ACsvfUf1TgCwuyF5_AkavALhxpYH_BDk?tab=summary&timestamp=2214.0)

### Ramp SEO Strategy Presentation

  - [**Goal:** 3 articles/week for 8 weeks (24 total), focusing on quality.](https://fathom.video/share/ACsvfUf1TgCwuyF5_AkavALhxpYH_BDk?tab=summary&timestamp=300.0)
  - [**Success Metrics:** Impressions, CTR, top rankings for prioritized terms, and AI/LLM citations.](https://fathom.video/share/ACsvfUf1TgCwuyF5_AkavALhxpYH_BDk?tab=summary&timestamp=329.0)
  - [**Core Insight:** Ramp is a mature domain with thousands of articles and a stronger organic presence than competitor Brex.](https://fathom.video/share/ACsvfUf1TgCwuyF5_AkavALhxpYH_BDk?tab=summary&timestamp=357.0)
  - [**Dual-Track Strategy:**](https://fathom.video/share/ACsvfUf1TgCwuyF5_AkavALhxpYH_BDk?tab=summary&timestamp=397.0)
    1.  [**Optimization (Short-Term Wins):** Refresh dated content (e.g., from 2022) and improve pages ranking 4–20 to capture top spots.](https://fathom.video/share/ACsvfUf1TgCwuyF5_AkavALhxpYH_BDk?tab=summary&timestamp=397.0)
    2.  [**Expansion (Long-Term Authority):** Build a new "CFO" thought leadership cluster to fill a content gap and target high-value decision-makers.](https://fathom.video/share/ACsvfUf1TgCwuyF5_AkavALhxpYH_BDk?tab=summary&timestamp=443.0)
  - [**Proposed Clusters & Topics:**](https://fathom.video/share/ACsvfUf1TgCwuyF5_AkavALhxpYH_BDk?tab=summary&timestamp=613.0)
      - [**Finance Operations:** Refresh existing content around high-intent terms like "spend optimization."](https://fathom.video/share/ACsvfUf1TgCwuyF5_AkavALhxpYH_BDk?tab=summary&timestamp=613.0)
      - [**Enterprise Procurement:** Deepen existing content to focus on the enterprise audience.](https://fathom.video/share/ACsvfUf1TgCwuyF5_AkavALhxpYH_BDk?tab=summary&timestamp=654.0)
      - [**CFOs:** Create a new cluster with topics like "90-day CFO playbook" and "CFO priorities" to build authority and capture backlinks.](https://fathom.video/share/ACsvfUf1TgCwuyF5_AkavALhxpYH_BDk?tab=summary&timestamp=680.0)

### Interview Discussion & Feedback

  - [**E-E-A-T & SME Expertise:**](https://fathom.video/share/ACsvfUf1TgCwuyF5_AkavALhxpYH_BDk?tab=summary&timestamp=725.0)
      - [**Rationale:** Expertise is mandatory for Ramp, a "Your Money, Your Life" (YMYL) site.](https://fathom.video/share/ACsvfUf1TgCwuyF5_AkavALhxpYH_BDk?tab=summary&timestamp=725.0)
      - [**Challenge:** Securing time from internal SMEs for content review is difficult.](https://fathom.video/share/ACsvfUf1TgCwuyF5_AkavALhxpYH_BDk?tab=summary&timestamp=725.0)
      - [**Workaround:** Use a ghostwriter with topical expertise and credit a high-visibility client team member to lend authority.](https://fathom.video/share/ACsvfUf1TgCwuyF5_AkavALhxpYH_BDk?tab=summary&timestamp=725.0)
  - [**AI Visibility (AIO/LLM):**](https://fathom.video/share/ACsvfUf1TgCwuyF5_AkavALhxpYH_BDk?tab=summary&timestamp=1194.0)
      - [**Approach:** Integrate AIO/LLM considerations into the standard content structure; it is not a separate strategy.](https://fathom.video/share/ACsvfUf1TgCwuyF5_AkavALhxpYH_BDk?tab=summary&timestamp=1194.0)
      - [**Feedback:** A baseline analysis of current AIO/LLM performance would strengthen the strategy.](https://fathom.video/share/ACsvfUf1TgCwuyF5_AkavALhxpYH_BDk?tab=summary&timestamp=2441.0)
  - [**Client Research Process:**](https://fathom.video/share/ACsvfUf1TgCwuyF5_AkavALhxpYH_BDk?tab=summary&timestamp=1764.0)
      - [**Method:** A hybrid of AI for data analysis (e.g., ranking distribution) and manual browsing for qualitative insights and gap validation.](https://fathom.video/share/ACsvfUf1TgCwuyF5_AkavALhxpYH_BDk?tab=summary&timestamp=1783.0)
  - [**Onboarding Process:**](https://fathom.video/share/ACsvfUf1TgCwuyF5_AkavALhxpYH_BDk?tab=summary&timestamp=2340.0)
      - [**Week 1:** Absorb documentation, learn the GrowthX platform, and understand the "Marcel way" (the CEO's strategic system).](https://fathom.video/share/ACsvfUf1TgCwuyF5_AkavALhxpYH_BDk?tab=summary&timestamp=2340.0)
      - [**Week 2+:** Shadow an Engagement Manager to observe client meetings and strategy building.](https://fathom.video/share/ACsvfUf1TgCwuyF5_AkavALhxpYH_BDk?tab=summary&timestamp=2391.0)

## Next Steps

  - [**GrowthX Team:** Discuss interview feedback and decide on next steps.](https://fathom.video/share/ACsvfUf1TgCwuyF5_AkavALhxpYH_BDk?tab=summary&timestamp=2647.0)
  - [**Viviane Ramos:** Check out GrowthX's free AI visibility tool.](https://fathom.video/share/ACsvfUf1TgCwuyF5_AkavALhxpYH_BDk?tab=summary&timestamp=2575.0)

action_items.description: Review GrowthX AI visibility tool (George's link) for Ramp; assess AIO/GEO baseline
